title: Arabic Writing and Pronunciation with Focus on Key Verbs and Particles
summary: The session reviewed the test results, highlighting common mistakes in writing Arabic words and mispronunciations. The teacher emphasized learning correct spelling rules, distinguishing letters (e.g., ṭā'/qāf), using tanwīn correctly, and introduced verbs like kharaja (to leave) with third-person masculine form. Students were encouraged to practice writing and recognizing letters.
key_points: Common test mistakes: omitting alif in طالبون, miswriting كلبون as قلبون, and not using تنوين
Differentiating Arabic letters like ṭā' (ط) and qāf (ق) to avoid misunderstandings (dog vs. heart)
Correct usage of prepositions مِن (from), إِلَىٰ (to/two), their impact on case endings (kasrah)
Introduction to verbs with kharaja (خرج) as third-person masculine past tense, discussing gender and number variations
questions: [00:16:00] Q: Why wasn't Mada taught before? — A: Mada belongs to istifham (question words), used specifically for questioning
[00:24:00] Q: Difference between إِلَىٰ and min with examples from Quran — A: مِن causes kasrah; إِلَىٰ is a separate preposition requiring alif maqṣūrah

quiz.q: What was a common writing mistake students made with the word طَالِبٌ (student)?
options: A) Writing ط as ق
B) Omitting the alif, writing طلب instead of طالب
C) Adding an extra نون at the end
D) Forgetting the تنوين
quiz.a: Students would leave out the alif, writing طلب instead of the correct طالب. The alif is essential and cannot be omitted.

quiz.q: In the lecture, writing كَلْبٌ (dog) as قَلْبٌ is a serious mistake because قَلْبٌ means something entirely different. What does قَلْبٌ mean?
options: A) Dog
B) Book
C) Heart
D) Door
quiz.a: قَلْبٌ means heart, while كَلْبٌ means dog. Confusing ك (kāf) with ق (qāf) completely changes the meaning of the word.

quiz.q: مِنْ and إِلَى belong to which grammatical family?
options: A) أَسْمَاءُ الْإِشَارَةِ (demonstrative nouns)
B) حُرُوفُ الْجَرِّ (prepositions/particles of kasra)
C) ضَمَائِر (pronouns)
D) أَدَوَاتُ الِاسْتِفْهَامِ (question words)
quiz.a: Both مِنْ and إِلَى are حُرُوفُ الْجَرِّ — prepositions that cause the following noun to take a kasra (ـِ) ending.

quiz.q: What grammatical effect do حُرُوفُ الْجَرِّ such as مِنْ, إِلَى, فِي, and عَلَى have on the word that follows them?
options: A) They add a ḍamma (ـُ) to the following word
B) They add a fatḥa (ـَ) to the following word
C) They add a kasra (ـِ) to the following word
D) They have no effect on the following word
quiz.a: All حُرُوفُ الْجَرِّ cause the noun that follows them to become majrūr — it takes a kasra (ـِ) ending, e.g., مِنَ الْبَيْتِ.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of أَنْتَ and which person/gender does it refer to?
options: A) I — first person singular
B) He — third person masculine singular
C) You — second person masculine singular
D) You — second person feminine singular
quiz.a: أَنْتَ means 'you' and is a second-person masculine singular pronoun. For females, the form أَنْتِ is used instead.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of أَنَا and what type of word is it?
options: A) He — a third-person pronoun (ضَمِير)
B) I — a first-person pronoun (ضَمِير)
C) You — a second-person pronoun (ضَمِير)
D) We — a first-person plural pronoun (ضَمِير)
quiz.a: أَنَا means 'I' and is a first-person singular pronoun (ضَمِير). It belongs to the ضَمَائِر family introduced in this lesson.

quiz.q: خَرَجَ is the first verb (فِعْل) introduced in this course. What does it mean and what form is it in?
options: A) He will leave — future tense, third person masculine singular
B) He left — past tense, third person masculine singular
C) I left — past tense, first person singular
D) They left — past tense, third person masculine plural
quiz.a: خَرَجَ means 'he left' and is past tense, third person masculine singular. Verbs in Arabic change form based on person, gender, and number.

quiz.q: A student asked: since خَرَجَ means 'he left,' should we say هُوَ خَرَجَ? What was the teacher's answer?
options: A) Yes, هُوَ must always be stated before a verb for clarity
B) No, because خَرَجَ already has the pronoun built into it, making هُوَ redundant
C) Yes, but only in formal writing
D) No, because هُوَ is only used with nouns, not verbs
quiz.a: The pronoun هُوَ (he) is embedded within خَرَجَ itself. Adding هُوَ separately is superfluous (extra), though not grammatically wrong for emphasis.

quiz.q: In Sūrah al-Isrāʾ (17:1), the phrase مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى uses both مِنْ and إِلَى. What do these two particles mean respectively?
options: A) In … on
B) From … to
C) To … from
D) On … in
quiz.a: مِنْ means 'from' and إِلَى means 'to.' The āyah describes Allāh عَزَّ وَجَلَّ taking the Prophet from al-Masjid al-Ḥarām to al-Masjid al-Aqṣā.

quiz.q: The plural verb form خَرَجُوا appears in Sūrah al-Baqara (2:243). How does this differ from the singular خَرَجَ?
options: A) خَرَجُوا is feminine singular; خَرَجَ is masculine singular
B) خَرَجُوا is past tense; خَرَجَ is future tense
C) خَرَجُوا is third person masculine plural (they left); خَرَجَ is third person masculine singular (he left)
D) They are the same form with no difference in meaning
quiz.a: خَرَجُوا means 'they left' (third person masculine plural), while خَرَجَ means 'he left' (third person masculine singular). Arabic verbs change based on number, gender, and person.

quran_examples.note: Illustrates مِنْ as a ḥarf al-jarr; الْجِنَّةِ takes kasra because it follows مِنَ.

quran_examples.arabic: سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى
quran_examples.transliteration: subḥāna lladhī asrā bi-ʿabdihī laylan mina l-masjidi l-ḥarāmi ilā l-masjidi l-aqṣā
quran_examples.translation: Glory be to the One Who took His servant by night from al-Masjid al-Ḥarām to al-Masjid al-Aqṣā
quran_examples.note: Both مِنَ (from) and إِلَى (to) appear together, demonstrating their opposite directional meanings and their role as ḥurūf al-jarr causing kasra on what follows.

quran_examples.translation: And each one is swimming in an orbit
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates فِي (in) as a ḥarf al-jarr; فَلَكٍ (orbit) takes kasra because it follows فِي. Used in class as an example of ḥurūf al-jarr in action.

quran_examples.translation: To whom belongs the dominion today?
quran_examples.note: Shows لِ as another ḥarf al-jarr alongside مِنْ, إِلَى, فِي, and عَلَى — all cause the following word to take kasra.

quran_examples.arabic: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحَابِ الْفِيلِ
quran_examples.transliteration: a-lam tara kayfa faʿala rabbuka bi-aṣḥābi l-fīl
quran_examples.translation: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the People of the Elephant?
quran_examples.note: Cited in the lecture; أَلَمْ تَرَ introduces a rhetorical question and كَيْفَ (how) belongs to the question-word (istifhām) family — contextually linking to the lesson on question words.

quran_examples.arabic: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِنْ دِيَارِهِمْ
quran_examples.transliteration: a-lam tara ilā lladhīna kharajū min diyārihim
quran_examples.translation: Have you not seen those who left their homes
quran_examples.note: Cited to illustrate خَرَجُوا — the plural form of the first verb خَرَجَ taught in this class — and simultaneously shows both إِلَى and مِنْ as ḥurūf al-jarr in a single āyah.
